coredata.dc:title: Light-emitting carbon dots extracted from naturally grown torreya grandis seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin-Yu
Zhuo, Ming-Peng
Liao, Liang-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), traditionally based on lead or cadmium, are the promising fluorescent materials for use in bright illumination, detecting and display technology. However, the toxicity of these QDs materials traditionally based on heavy metal cause great concern to human health and environmental protection. Thus, carbon dots from pure natural materials with low toxicity and extensive resources soon raised attention. Here, we introduce a pure physical method to extract carbon dots from the seed's inner skin of torreya grandis which is a Chinese endemic plant widely grown in the southeastern China, such as in Jiangsu, Fujian and Zhejiang Provinces. These carbon dots come from natural plants and only cyclohexane and ethanol with hypotoxicity are used in extracting process to ensure the low toxicity of our material. Besides, we also fabricated light-emitting diodes based on the extracted light-emitting carbon dots. The emission peak of our devices appears at 438 nm with Commission International de I'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate (0.177, 0.075).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Torreya grandis seeds
Fluorescence
Pure natural

Performance of torreya carbon dots based LEDs
Structure of torreya carbon dots based LEDs

This project is also funded by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology (Nano-CIC), and the 111 Project of The State Administration of Foreign Experts Affairs of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The flow chart of physical synthetic route of carbon dots from torreya grandis seeds.

a) PL spectra of torreya carbon dots dispersed in ethanol solvent with different excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 340 nm. Picture of torreya carbon dots solution with centrifugation speed of 6000 rpm and 12,000 rpm under the 345 nm excitation wavelength of UV light (inset), b) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of torreya carbon dots.

a) AFM image of pure carbon dots, b) XRD pattern of carbon dots, c) C 1s XPS spectrum of CDots, d) O 1s XPS spectrum of CDots, e) FTIR spectrum of carbon dots. Inset: Structure of torreya-grandate molecule.

a) AFM picture of the light-emitting layer, b) the schematic of energy levels of different layers in the devices, c) EL spectrum of the device under 6 V operational voltage. Upper left inset: Picture of device when working; Upper right inset: CIE coordinate of the device, d) current density and luminance vs. voltage of devices, e) current efficiency and EQE of devices, f) lifetime measurement of the device.
Comparison of our carbon-dot LEDs with other LEDs.

head.ce:title: Light-emitting carbon dots extracted from naturally grown torreya grandis seeds

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), traditionally based on lead or cadmium, are the promising fluorescent materials for use in bright illumination, detecting and display technology. However, the toxicity of these QDs materials traditionally based on heavy metal cause great concern to human health and environmental protection. Thus, carbon dots from pure natural materials with low toxicity and extensive resources soon raised attention. Here, we introduce a pure physical method to extract carbon dots from the seed's inner skin of torreya grandis which is a Chinese endemic plant widely grown in the southeastern China, such as in Jiangsu, Fujian and Zhejiang Provinces. These carbon dots come from natural plants and only cyclohexane and ethanol with hypotoxicity are used in extracting process to ensure the low toxicity of our material. Besides, we also fabricated light-emitting diodes based on the extracted light-emitting carbon dots. The emission peak of our devices appears at 438 nm with Commission International de I'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate (0.177, 0.075).

ce:section.ce:para: Since Brus and his colleagues first put up the concept of colloidal quantum dots [1], this kind of magical material mainly based on lead and cadmium was considered as the most promising material in many fields, such as bright illumination, detecting, and display technology [2–11]. However, the European Union revised the RoHS (The Restriction of the use of Certain Hazardous Substances in Electrical and Electronic Equipment) draft in 2013 to restrict the use of cadmium, lead and mercury based materials in electrical and electronic equipment. The extreme toxicity of not only those traditional QDs, but also lead based perovskite materials raised concern of the whole society and thus added a further impediment of their applications [12,13]. Recently, much progress has been made in studies of carbon dots due to its non-toxic material resource. Much work has been done in designing and synthesizing light-emitting carbon dots with high efficiency and tunable wavelength as well as in fabricating carbon dot based light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with emission peaks ranging from violet to near-infrared (NIR) [14–25].

In addition, extensive resource of raw materials is another attractive aspect of carbon dots. Carbon as one of the most abundant elements on earth, is much easier to collect compared with heavy metal elements including lead and cadmium. As the most important structural element of organism, carbon dots can even be synthesized from hair and plant tissues [26,27]. Mostly, carbon dots are synthesized by hydrothermal method and afterwards with complex chemical treatment. Chlorinated organics and other toxic solvents are inevitably used during the subsequent chemical treatment process. Thus, it cannot be considered as a completely safe and non-toxic synthetic method.

Here, we report a totally physical method to extract carbon dots from a pure natural raw material, torreya grandis seeds. Torreya grandis is a Chinese endemic plant widely grow in the southeastern China such as in Jiangsu, Fujian and Zhejiang Provinces. We collect the black aril (or inner skin) of its cooked seeds and successfully extract carbon dots from it physically. The carbon dots show a strong photoluminescence (PL) peak located at 431 nm with the absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) of 13.7%. In order to prevent aggregation-induced emission quenching and improve the morphology of light emitting layer, the carbon dots were then mixed with poly vinyl carbazole (PVK) together as the light emitting layer in our LEDs. The electroluminescence (EL) peak of our device appears at about 438 nm with Commission International de I'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate of (0.177, 0.075). Since the raw material is totally nontoxic and the non-use of halogen-based organic solvents like chlorobenzene or chloroform in the whole extracting process, this material is hopefully to be applicable into bio-probes and some other biological products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracting method and properties of torreya carbon dots
Physical method of extracting torreya carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A branch of a torreya grandis plant and the fresh fruit are shown in Figs. S1a and S1b, respectively. The fresh torreya fruit has a green peel which wraps a dark brown color hard seed coat. Shown in Fig. S1c are the cooked seeds. Between the hard coat and the edible pip is a layer of black aril (or inner skin). We first collect the black aril and grind it with a mortar for 2 h. The powder of aril is then dispersed in cyclohexane and centrifuged at 6000 rpm in order to clean the fat. The powder is ground again for 1 h after drying at vacuum to make it into smaller particles. Finally, we use the tissue grinding system to break the powder particle into extreme small size. The powder is centrifuged again in cyclohexane at 6000 rpm. After drying at vacuum, we disperse the powder in ethanol and centrifuge it at 12,000 rpm. The schematic of whole process is shown in Fig. 1 . The prepared solution is stored for testing (inset of Fig. 2 a).

The torreya carbon dots emits strong blue light with PL emission peak centered at 431 nm (Fig. 2a) with an absolute PLQY of 13.7%. It has a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 45 nm, which is smaller than that of 80 nm from the recently reported blue emission carbon dots [28,29]. When changing excitation wavelength, the emission peak of carbon dots has no obvious red-shift or blue-shift. and keeps its FWHM as narrow as 45 nm. It demonstrates stable band-gap emission properties of the torreya carbon dots. The carbon dot films show good PL stability under different heating temperatures (120 °C, 160 °C and 200 °C) and heating time; the PL spectra shape and wavelength show little change under continuous heating. While the carbon dots show resistance to the acid conditions (pH = 3), the base conditions indeed damage the carbon dots, evidenced by the spectral broadening and the shoulder intensity increasing, as is shown in Fig. S2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2b) shows that carbon dots are dispersed well in the layer with a similar size around 4.88 nm averaged among 200 particles (inset of Fig. 2b). The inter-planar spacing of 0.21 nm is clearly showed in the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2b). The atomic force microscope (AFM) image with pure carbon dots is shown in Fig. 3 a. It shows rough and inhomogeneous morphology. This explains the necessity of doping the carbon dots into PVK to ensure smooth film in our device in the following experiment. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data (Fig. 3b) show typical carbon dots peaks that agree with literature [30]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of C 1s and O 1s are shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. By calculating the area of the two peaks, the ratio of C element to O element turns out to be 6:1. Besides, we also tested the fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of torreya carbon dots to further identify the molecular structure of the carbon dots. As is listed in Fig. 3e, the carbon dots exhibit O–H (3430 cm−1) stretching vibration, C–H (2960, 2923, 2860 cm−1) stretching vibration, C6H5- (1627, 1530 cm−1) stretching vibration, CH(CH3)2- (1385, 1130 cm−1) stretching vibration, and C–O (1025 cm−1) bending vibration. It roughly matches the FTIR spectrum of torreya grandate [31]. The molecular structure of torreyagrandate is indicated in Fig. 3e as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of torreya carbon dots based LEDs
Structure of torreya carbon dots based LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: We use patterned indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrate as the anode, poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) as a hole-injecting layer, poly (9,9-dioctylfluorene-coN-(4-(3-methylpropyl)) diphenylamine) (TFB) as a hole-transporting layer, torreya carbon dots (CQDs) doped poly (vinyl carbazole) (PVK) as a light-emitting layer, 2, 2′, 2"- (1, 3, 5- Benzinetriyl)- tris (1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi) as an electron-transporting layer, 8-Quinolinolato lithium (Liq) as an electron-injecting layer, and aluminium (Al) as a cathode (As shown in Fig. S3). We use PVK as host material in order to disperse the carbon dots better to form a uniform film and prevent aggregation-induced emission quenching. Atomic force microscope (AFM) measurements shows the light-emitting layer has a small root-mean square roughness less than 0.3 nm (Fig. 4 a).

Fig. 4b shows the schematic of energy levels of the multilayer devices. From the ITO side, the energy gap of LUMO is suitable enough for high-efficiency hole transferring into the light-emitting layer. From the aluminum cathode side, electrons can easily transfer to light-emitting layer due to small energy gap of 0.4 eV of HOMO. At the light-emitting layer, HOMO and LUMO of carbon dots are between those of the PVK. According to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), energy can easily transfer from PVK to carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performances of torreya carbon dots based LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since torreya carbon dots are synthesized totally by physical method without any chemical reaction and surface engineering, we speculate the devices would have rather poor electroluminescence properties because of its possibly poor charge transfer and high density of surface traps. However, we surprisingly find that the devices have satisfied performances. As is shown in Fig. 4c, the deep-blue emission peak of QLED devices appears to be centered at 438 nm, 7 nm red-shifted compared with the PL emission peak. Commission International de I'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate of the devices is located at (0.177, 0.075) which falls in the range of purplish-blue area according to CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. Compared with the reported PVK emission peak at 390 nm [25], we can infer that the 438 nm blue EL emission comes from torreya carbon dots instead of the PVK host material. The turn-on voltage turns out to be around 5.5 V which indicates a relatively low resistance of the devices. When working under 6 V, the luminance is tested to be 32 cd/m2 and can be easily observed by naked eyes in daylight. The maximum EQE is 0.55% with very low roll-off when voltage goes on. It means that good charge transfer property and little leak current have been achieved in the devices. We compared our devices with perovskite LEDs, ZnCdS QD LEDs and the other carbon LEDs, and summarized the performance in Table 1 . Also, we measured the T50 (time of luminance dropping to 50%) of our device under an initial luminance of 32 cd/m2. The result is shown in Fig. 4e. The T50 (65 h) can be further improved by surface passivation of the quantum dots [32,33].

We successfully extracted carbon dots from seed of torreya grandis by purely physical method without any chemical treatment and surface engineering. The carbon dots can emit blue light with wavelength at 431 nm. The average diameter of the carbon dots is around 4.88 nm. The LEDs based on torreya carbon dots were fabricated for further studies. The EL peak of the devices is located at 438 nm, 7 nm red-shifted compared with the PL emission peak. The CIE color coordinate of the devices is located at (0.177, 0.075) which falls in the range of purplish-blue area according to CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. Our work paved a new way in extracting quantum dots from natural raw materials of organism with totally non-toxic route with non-use of halogen-based organic solvents like chlorobenzene or chloroform. We hope this kind of light-emitting quantum dots could also be applicable in biological products including bio-probe and bio-sensor.

The cooked torreya grandis seeds were purchased from local market. The cyclohexane and ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. PEDOT:PSS was purchased from Xi’ an Polymer Light Technology Corp. TFB and Liq were purchased from Luminescence Technology. PVK was purchased from Acros. All the materials were directly used without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical synthetic route of torreya carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Black aril were collected by handwork. We first ground the sheet-like aril with a mortar for 2 h to break it into powder. The powder of aril was then dispersed in cyclohexane and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. We removed the liquid and dried the powder at vacuum. The powder was ground again for 1 more hour to make it into smaller particles. Tissue grinding system (FastPrep-24 ™ 5G Instrument) was then used to break the powder particle into extreme small size. The powder was centrifuged again in cyclohexane at 6000 rpm for 10 min. After drying at vacuum, we dispersed the powder in ethanol and centrifuge it at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The prepared solution was stored for testing.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image were collected by FEI-Titan G2 80–200 Chemi scanning transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was collected by Hyperion spectrophotometer (Bruker) after using standard KBr pellet method at the scan range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The PL spectra were obtained by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 340 nm.

The devices were fabricated on indium tin oxide (ITO) based glass substrates. The surface of ITO glass was carefully cleaned for 2–4 times to reduce the impurities by detergent and deionized water. Ultrasonically cleaning with acetone and ethanol orderly for 3–4 times was required. After drying at vacuum, UV ozone treatment in air for 15 min was taken to optimize ITO work function and to decrease the potential barriers. PEDOT: PSS as hole-transporting layer (HTL) was dispersed onto the pre-treated ITO glass substrate by spin-coating at 4000 rpm for 40 s followed with annealing at 120 °C for 10 min in air. Then, ITO glass with PEDOT: PSS was transferred into a nitrogen-filled glovebox. TFB was spin-coated on the device at 6000 rpm for 40 s. Annealing at 120 °C for 10 min was required. Afterwards, the carbon dots/PVK choloride benzene solution was spin-coated onto the TFB layer at 4000 rpm for 40 s, followed by annealing at 150 °C for 10 min. TPBi, Liq and cathode of aluminium were prepared in a thermal evaporator at high vacuum with pressure below 4 × 10−6 Torr. The devices were encapsulated with a cover glass by UV glue in the glovebox in order to reduce the influence of oxygen and water.

The J–V characteristics, electroluminescence spectra, CE and EQE were measured with Keithley 2400 source meter, photometer (Photo Research PR 655 spectrophotometer) and integrating sphere (Hamamatsu A10094). Atomic force microscope (AFM) image was obtained by using a Cypher-S atomic force microscope.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61961160731, 51773141, and 51821002). This project is also funded by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology (Nano-CIC), and the 111 Project of The State Administration of Foreign Experts Affairs of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.orgel.2021.106255.
